content:
Frontier Models Were Trained on All of Human Knowledge. The Gains Go to Six Companies. That’s a Policy Choice.

Who owns the value when AI is built on everyone’s work? Capability thresholds, regulatory capture, the commons problem, and why voluntary commitments are theater.

10 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Here’s a thought experiment.
Imagine every journalist, researcher, novelist, programmer, doctor, and blogger who ever published anything online walked into a room together. Now imagine a company told them: “We scraped everything you ever wrote, trained a system on it, and built a $2 trillion business. You get nothing. Also, some of you are fired now.”
That’s not a thought experiment. That happened.
Six firms — OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, Meta, Microsoft, and xAI — hold combined market capitalizations exceeding $12 trillion. They built that value on a specific foundation: the collective output of human civilization. Every article, paper, codebase, novel, medical study, legal brief, and Reddit comment. Scraped at scale. Tokenized. Compressed into model weights.
The people who created that data received nothing. Many are now being displaced by systems trained on their work.
This is not an inevitable outcome. It’s a policy choice made by default. And every element of it can be chosen differently.
This is Part 8 of the AI Policy series. Part 7 examined who bears the costs of AI displacement. This article asks the mirror question: who captures the value that produces it?

We’ve Seen This Movie Before
The “we took the risk” argument is familiar. Oil companies said it about drilling on public land. Railroads said it about building on public land grants. Both were right — and we still regulated them.
The frontier labs’ version: “We invested tens of billions in compute. We hired the researchers. We took financial risk. Training data is transformed, not copied. Fair use.”
These are real arguments. They deserve honest engagement.
But they’re incomplete. Political economy asks a different question than intellectual property law: when displacement costs fall on workers and communities while returns concentrate in six firms, what public authority is justified?
The answer hinges on a framing choice. Are frontier models:
A) Products → Regulate for safety (the lighter touch) B) Infrastructure built on commons → Regulate for distribution (the heavier hand)
The framing you choose determines the entire policy architecture that follows. Most current governance defaults to (A). The evidence increasingly supports (B).

The 10²⁶ FLOP Line in the Sand (That’s Already Underwater)
Executive Order 14110 (October 2023) drew the first real line: if your training run exceeds 10²⁶ FLOPs, you trigger oversight. Report to Commerce. Do safety evaluations. Let NIST develop benchmarks.
The logic is clean: target risk by capability, not by corporate identity. Small firms doing safe work face no burden. Only frontier-scale models trigger scrutiny.
Here’s the problem: that line is already obsolete.
The 10²⁶ FLOP threshold was frontier in 2023. By 2025, mid-tier labs and well-funded startups blew past it. Who updates the threshold? NIST can develop benchmarks, but the political process for adjusting numerical thresholds lags technical progress by years.
Worse: compute is an increasingly bad proxy for capability. Better algorithms, mixture-of-experts architectures, improved data curation, and synthetic data pipelines all reduce the compute needed for equivalent performance. You can build a more capable model with less compute every quarter.
And enforcement? Self-reporting. No independent verification of compute usage during training. It’s the honor system for trillion-dollar companies.

The Licensing Alternative (And Why It’s Also Broken)
If capability thresholds degrade, what about licensing the organizations instead? That’s roughly the EU AI Act approach: license entities, impose ongoing compliance, conduct periodic audits.
This creates real accountability. Institutional relationships. Audit trails.
It also massively favors incumbents. Compliance costs are largely fixed, meaning they’re proportionally cheaper for firms with billions in revenue. A regime designed to prevent concentration risks entrenching exactly the concentration it should address.
The tension is genuine and probably unresolvable with either approach alone. Effective oversight likely requires both: capability thresholds for triggering enhanced scrutiny, and institutional relationships for ongoing accountability.
Neither alone solves the problem. Together they create overlapping coverage that’s harder to game.

The Commons Problem (This Is the Core of Everything)
Strip away the technical details and here’s what happened:
Human civilization produced a commons — the internet, basically. Billions of people contributed knowledge, creativity, analysis, and expertise over three decades. Then six companies vacuumed it up and monetized it at a scale none of the contributors imagined or consented to.
Now the legal system is trying to figure out if that was okay.
The Lawsuits That Will Shape Everything
The New York Times v. OpenAI. The Authors Guild class action. Getty Images v. Stability AI. These cases will determine whether training on copyrighted data constitutes fair use at scale.
If courts say yes (fair use): Copyright law won’t constrain training. Governance has to work through other mechanisms — compute oversight, distribution requirements, public benefit obligations.
If courts say no (not fair use): Licensing frameworks become mandatory. The commons argument shifts from moral claim to legal obligation.
Either way, the policy question remains: how do you distribute value that was created collectively but captured privately?
Why Individual Compensation Doesn’t Work
Jaron Lanier’s “data dignity” concept — micropayments for data contributions — is intellectually elegant and practically impossible. You can’t meaningfully say a specific journalist’s articles account for 0.0001% of GPT-5’s capability. The contribution isn’t linear or separable.
The tractable approach is collective, not individual. Tax the training compute or the revenue derived from it. Redistribute through public benefit mechanisms. This looks more like a severance tax on extraction than a royalty on individual contributions.
We already know how to do this. We just haven’t chosen to.

Regulatory Capture Isn’t a Risk. It’s the Default.
Every time someone proposes AI oversight, someone else warns about regulatory capture. Here’s the thing: capture isn’t a risk to manage. It’s the outcome you get unless you deliberately design against it.
The expertise asymmetry alone makes capture nearly inevitable. Frontier labs employ the people who understand these systems. Regulators don’t. The revolving door operates through a tiny pool of qualified evaluators rotating between labs and government, building shared assumptions and aligned incentives.
This Has Happened Before
AT&T wrote telecom policy for decades. Until antitrust breakup in 1982.
Dodd-Frank compliance costs ($10B+ annually for the industry) favor large banks because they’re mostly fixed costs. The regulation designed to prevent “too big to fail” may have entrenched it.
What Actually Mitigates Capture
Known solutions, rarely implemented at the moment institutions are created:

• Independent funding not dependent on industry fees

• Mandatory public interest representation with real authority, not advisory window dressing

• Three-year cooling-off periods for industry-to-regulator moves

• Open evaluation frameworks with publicly auditable benchmarks

• Sunset clauses forcing periodic reauthorization
The AI Safety Institute (AISI) within NIST is a partial attempt. But it operates with a fraction of any single frontier lab’s resources, and its mandate remains advisory, not enforcement.
The structural question nobody wants to answer: can AISI build genuine independence, or is it another venue for industry influence wearing institutional clothing?

The “Open-Source” Shell Game
Meta releases Llama openly. This is genuinely good for competition, research, auditing, and startup ecosystems.
But let’s be precise about what “open-weight” means in governance terms: Meta still controls training decisions, captures ecosystem value, and determines base capabilities. The community can fine-tune and deploy. The foundational choices remain private. “Open-weight” is not “open-source” in any meaningful governance sense.
The Real Policy Tension
For open models: Without them, only incumbents can build at scale. Open availability enables independent auditing. Academic access depends on it. Competition constrains closed providers.
Against unlimited openness: Open models can be fine-tuned to remove safety constraints. Once weights are released, there’s no recall mechanism. Liability becomes diffuse.
The design question: Is tiered openness achievable? Open for research, restricted for commercial deployment above certain capability levels?
Technically feasible. Politically brutal. Closed-model providers want lower thresholds (restricts competition). Open-model advocates want no thresholds (maximizes access). The optimal policy lives in a space neither side will voluntarily occupy.

Voluntary Commitments Are Theater
The White House voluntary commitments of July 2023: no enforcement mechanism. No penalties. No independent verification. Companies self-report on safety.
That’s not oversight. It’s a press release with a longer shelf life.
Voluntary commitments fail structurally because they:

• Impose no binding force (firms withdraw when compliance costs rise)

• Rely on self-reporting with no audit capacity

• Punish restraint (the firm that slows for safety loses to the firm that doesn’t)

• Provide no update mechanism as capabilities advance
The analogy: pre-EPA environmental self-regulation. Rivers caught fire before we established enforceable standards. We’re in the “rivers on fire” equivalent right now, except the damage is economic displacement rather than literal combustion.
The Enforcement Toolkit That Doesn’t Exist Yet
What real oversight would look like:

• Pre-deployment review with mandatory waiting periods above capability thresholds

• Independent evaluation labs with access to model internals, not just API endpoints

• Mandatory incident reporting with liability for concealment

• Financial penalties scaled to revenue, not flat fines that become cost of business

• License revocation for repeated non-compliance
None of this exists. All of it is technically achievable.

Should Some AI Be Public Infrastructure?
Here’s the question nobody in Washington asks: if training data is a commons, and models built on it impose social costs while generating private returns, should some frontier AI capability be governed as public infrastructure?
The Honest Case For
Public investment already subsidized the foundations: NSF grants, DARPA funding, university research, publicly funded internet infrastructure. GPS is public infrastructure. So are roads, research universities, and the internet itself.
Democratic accountability for systems that affect everyone seems proportional to the public inputs that enabled them.
The Honest Case Against
Government moves slowly. Talent concentrates in private labs because compensation runs 5–10x government scale. Political interference would shape a public model in ways that undermine utility. Training costs hundreds of millions, and procurement processes can’t keep pace. International competition creates pressure public governance may not handle.
The Middle Ground That’s Actually Achievable
A fully public frontier model isn’t realistic within American political economy. But public infrastructure for evaluation, compute access, and safety testing is.
The realistic path:

• Public compute for researchers, startups, and civil society (without government building frontier models)
